title: Replacing a kitchen mixer tap
subtitle: Swap a single-lever or twin-lever mixer tap on a standard kitchen sink
excerpt: How to remove a kitchen mixer tap and fit a new one. Covers isolating supply, undoing seized back-nuts in a tight cupboard, fitting flexible tails, and bedding the new tap so the spout sits central over the bowl.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: The brass nut under the sink that clamps a tap onto the deck. Sits in tight space behind the sink bowl and needs a basin wrench to undo cleanly.

Stainless-braided flexible hoses with brass end fittings that connect the tap thread to the supply pipes. Replace the rigid copper tails on older taps and make the fit much easier in a tight cupboard.

content.text: A kitchen mixer tap is the most common plumbing swap in a house. The new tap arrives with
flexible tap tails
already fitted, which makes the connection in the cupboard much easier than the old rigid-tail tap. The slow part is the
back-nut
under the sink, which is usually seized.
Step 1, isolate the water and drain the section

Look under the sink for two isolating valves on the supply pipes. Turn the slot on each valve a quarter-turn with a flat screwdriver, so the slot lies across the pipe. If there are no isolating valves, close the stopcock under the kitchen sink. Open both taps and run them until the flow stops. Place a small bowl and a thick towel under the tap connections. A short pipe drains about 100 ml of residual water.
Step 2, disconnect the old tails

Undo the compression nut joining each tail to the supply pipe with the adjustable spanner. Hold the supply pipe with a second spanner so it does not twist. The nuts are brass and the pipe is copper; brass is softer and rounds quickly if forced. If the nut will not move, work a few drops of penetrating oil into the thread and wait 10 minutes.
Step 3, undo the back-nut

Reach up behind the bowl with the basin wrench. The jaws of the wrench clip over the back-nut from one side. Lever the handle slowly. The back-nut on an old tap may not move at first; work it back and forth a quarter-turn each way to break the corrosion bond before unscrewing fully. Lift the old tap clear once the back-nut is off.
Step 4, fit the new tap

Wrap three turns of PTFE tape around the tap thread in the direction of tightening. Place the new rubber washer over the thread on top of the sink. Lower the tap into the hole. Underneath, fit the second rubber washer, then the brass plate, then the back-nut. Hand-tighten the back-nut, then snug it with the basin wrench. The tap should sit firm on the deck without movement. Do not over-tighten; a stainless sink deforms under torque and the tap rocks on a curved deck.
Step 5, connect the flexible tails

Screw each flexible tail onto its supply pipe by hand. The cone seal inside the tail does the work; PTFE tape is not needed on flexible tail joints. Snug with the spanner, holding the supply pipe with the second spanner. A flexible tail seal needs only firm hand pressure plus a quarter-turn.
Step 6, restore supply and check for drips

Open the isolating valves slowly. Run both hot and cold for a minute each. Check every joint under the sink with a dry tissue paper. A damp mark on the tissue means a slow weep. Tighten the offending joint a sixth of a turn at a time until the weep stops. Run the tap full-bore on each setting to flush out PTFE shreds and air.
Variations

On a single-hole mixer the procedure is identical. On a twin-hole or three-hole deck mixer, the back-nut and tails are the same on each side, but the central body must be aligned before the back-nuts are tightened. Place a spirit level across the spout to check the body is square to the sink edge before final tightening.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Weep at the back-nut under the sink after the job
attrs.cause: The rubber washer under the deck slipped during fitting, or the back-nut is not square on the thread.
attrs.fix: Drain the section, slacken the back-nut a full turn, lift the tap free, check the washer is flat and centred, then refit. Tighten the back-nut by hand first to feel for cross-threading.

attrs.symptom: Flow is much lower on the hot than the cold
attrs.cause: Sediment in the flexible tail or the spout aerator was disturbed and is now blocking the hot side.
attrs.fix: Close the hot isolating valve. Undo the hot tail from the supply pipe. Run a small bucket of water through it from the tap end. Refit. If the flow is still low, unscrew the spout aerator and clean it under running water.

attrs.symptom: The basin wrench will not grip the old back-nut
attrs.cause: The back-nut has corroded round at the corners, or the gap behind the sink is too tight for the wrench jaws.
attrs.fix: Cut the back-nut with a junior hacksaw. Two cuts on opposite sides of the nut allow the halves to be cracked off with a screwdriver. The new tap supplies a new back-nut so the cut one is no loss.
